entries.content:
What is TheFloorr

TheFloorr is a luxury fashion personal styling platform. It connects professional stylists with fashion-conscious clients. The platform does not sell products directly. Instead, it earns revenue through affiliate commissions when stylists purchase products from retail partners via trackable links generated on the platform.

Stylists on TheFloorr create curated inspiration collections called "Moodboards." Moodboards can be shared for public access. When a purchase is made through a TheFloorr link, the affiliate network tracks the sale, and both TheFloorr and the stylist earn a commission.

Stylists (FRI — Fashion Retail Individual, PSE - Personal Shopper Entrepreneur, GPS - Guest Personal Shopper)

From system's perspective, FRI, GPS and PSE are the same thing. FRI and GPS are deprecated names, not used to describe stylists anymore.

Previously stylists were able to create "Looks" and "Consultations" (also called "Styling Session"). This feature is deprecated and no longer available in the system.

PSE's communication with clients is also deprecated. 

Currently, PSEs are purchasing products themselves or sharing trackable links and moodboards publicly.

Admins

TheFloorr administrators have full system access. They manage user accounts, approve PSE applications, handle payment disputes, configure retailer commission rates, manually adjust order statuses and payments, and access reporting dashboards. Admins can override any automatically calculated status on an order when corrections are needed.

Brands and Retailers

Retailers are the partner stores that list their products on TheFloorr catalogue through affiliate networks. They include luxury fashion houses and department stores. Retailers set their own commission rates for TheFloorr, which can differ between full-price and sale items, and can vary by region. Some retailers exclude certain product categories or VIP orders from commission.

Where Products Come From

TheFloorr does not hold inventory. All products come from three affiliate networks: Rakuten, Commission Junction (CJ), and Partnerize. These networks provide product data feeds from hundreds of retailers. The feeds are downloaded multiple times a day through an automated pipeline.

Product Structure

Each product has a name, brand/designer, description, images, and pricing. Pricing includes regular price and sale price, tracked per variant and per region. Products are categorised hierarchically (for example, Women > Dresses > Cocktail) and tagged with designer/brand information.

Regional and Gender Partitioning

Products are organised into sixteen search partitions based on gender (female, male) and region (UK, US, EU, Rest of World), with separate partitions for full-price and sale items. This means the same product may appear with different pricing depending on the customer's region.

Product Discovery

Customers and stylists can find products through text search or by filtering on designer, category, colour, retailer, price range, and sale status. Products can be sorted by newest or price.

Product Lifecycle

A product enters the system when imported from a feed. It becomes searchable and available for stylists to include in Moodboards. When a user clicks a product's trackable link and purchases it at the retailer, the affiliate network records the sale. If a product is removed from the feed, it is marked as deleted and removed from search results.

Designers and Brand Management

Each product is associated with a designer or brand. Admins can feature certain designers, exclude designers from commission, or ban designers entirely from the catalogue.

Commission on Products

Commission rates vary by retailer and by product type. Some retailers pay different rates for full-price versus sale items. Some retailers (notably Net-a-Porter) do not pay commission on VIP customer orders. Commission exclusions can also be set at the designer level.

Moodboards

Moodboards are curated product collections used for inspiration. Stylists create them to organise ideas for clients. Moodboards can be public or private, shared with specific users, and used as templates for teams. They contain products from the catalogue along with notes and inspiration.

How an Order Happens

Orders on TheFloorr are not placed on the platform itself. The purchase happens at the retail partner's website. The sequence is:

1. A stylist clicks a product's trackable affiliate link on TheFloorr or someone else clicks trackable link or a link in publicly shared Moodboard
2. The click is recorded, linking the user, the product, the retailer, and (if applicable) the stylist who recommended it
3. The person (stylist or client) completes the purchase on the retailer's website
4. The retailer reports the sale to the affiliate network (typically within 24-48 hours)
5. The affiliate network sends the order data to TheFloorr
6. TheFloorr creates an internal order record and attributes it to the click and the stylist

Attribution

The connection between a purchase and TheFloorr is called attribution. When someone clicks a trackable link, TheFloorr records which product was clicked, who clicked the link and who created the link. If the product is bought, the affiliate network matches the sale back to that click, and TheFloorr can attribute the order to the right stylist for commission purposes.

If an order comes in but cannot be matched to a specific stylist or click, the commission goes entirely to TheFloorr rather than being split with a stylist.

Order Status — Three Independent Dimensions

Every order has three separate status tracks that move independently:

**Affiliate Order Status** reflects what the affiliate network reports about the order. It can be pending (not yet confirmed), new, approved, auto-approved, returned, declined, closed, locked, or mixed (when different items have different statuses). Each network has slightly different status handling. For Rakuten, there is no explicit approval — an order is considered approved when Rakuten pays the commission. If you see "rejected" as order or order item status - it means "returned". 

**Internal Order Status** is TheFloorr's own assessment of the order. It is calculated from the affiliate status and the state of individual items. If all items have been returned, the order is internally marked as returned. If the network says the order is closed or locked, it is internally approved. If the order is still new or pending at the network level, it stays pending internally.

**Manual Status** is an admin override. If set, it forces the internal status to whatever the admin specifies, regardless of what the network says or what the item-level data shows. This is used for data corrections, reconciliation, and handling edge cases.

Order Items

Each order can contain multiple items, and each item can have its own status. An item can be approved while another item on the same order has been returned. Items track their own commission amounts, sale values, and payment statuses.

When a customer returns one item from a multi-item order, only that item's commission is affected. The order-level amounts are recalculated: the gross sale amount stays the same (representing the original order), but the net sale amount decreases by the returned item's value.

Returns and Refunds

Returns are detected when the affiliate network reports changes to previously confirmed orders. The process works like this:

1. A customer returns an item to the retailer
2. The retailer reports the return to the affiliate network
3. The network reduces the commission and updates statuses in next reports to TheFloorr
4. TheFloorr detects the changes and updates the order accordingly
5. The refund status is marked as pending if return happened after affiliate network already paid commission to TheFloorr.

Networks do not send money back to TheFloorr for returns. Instead, they deduct the returned commission from future payments. This means if TheFloorr was owed 100 pounds in commission next month but a previous order had a 20 pound return (after network payment), the network would pay 80 pounds.

Why an Order Might Stay Pending

Orders remain pending when the affiliate network has not yet confirmed them. Different networks have different confirmation timescales. Some orders sit in pending for weeks while the network validates the transaction and confirms the retailer has shipped the goods and the return window has passed.

Three Payment Streams

Each order involves up to three separate payment streams that operate independently:

**1. Network Payment (Affiliate Network → TheFloorr)**

The affiliate network pays TheFloorr the commission for approved orders. Each network has its own payment schedule. The payment status tracks whether the network has paid: pending means awaiting payment, paid means the money has been transferred. Rakuten, CJ, and Partnerize each have their own payment record tables since their data formats differ.

**2. PSE Payment (TheFloorr → Stylist)**

When an order is attributed to a stylist, that stylist's share of the commission needs to be paid out. The default split is 80% of the commission to the stylist and 20% retained by TheFloorr, but this rate is configurable per stylist and per retailer.

The PSE payment goes through several stages:
- Commission is calculated when the order is approved
- An admin or the system approves the commission for payment, creating an "approved payment" record
- Multiple approved payments are batched into a single Revolut payment draft
- The draft moves through states: approved, created in Revolut, sent, then completed or failed
- The stylist receives the money via bank transfer through Revolut

If the stylist is VAT-registered in the UK, VAT is added on top of their commission.

**3. TheFloorr Revenue**

TheFloorr keeps the portion of commission not paid to stylists. For orders without stylist attribution, TheFloorr keeps the entire commission.

Why PSE Payment Might Be Delayed

Stylist payments can be delayed because:
- The network has not yet paid TheFloorr (the order's affiliate payment status is still pending)
- The order is still pending approval at the network level
- The commission has not yet been approved for payment in the admin system
- The Revolut payment draft has not been created or sent yet

Commission Calculation Example

A client purchases a 500 pound dress through a TheFloorr link. The retailer pays 10% commission through the CJ network.

- Total commission: 50 pounds
- Stylist's share (80%): 40 pounds
- TheFloorr's share (20%): 10 pounds

If the client later returns the dress, the CJ network deducts 50 pounds from the next month's payment to TheFloorr. The stylist's 40 pound payment, if not yet paid, is cancelled. If already paid, the overpayment is tracked against the stylist's balance for future reconciliation.

Currency Handling

Orders are recorded in whatever currency the affiliate network reports (USD, GBP, EUR, JPY, etc.). All amounts are stored in the original currency. Conversion to a common currency happens on demand using exchange rates from the order date, payment date, or current date as needed.

Manual Adjustments

Admins can manually override virtually any calculated financial field on an order. This includes the internal status, payment statuses for all three streams, commission amounts, and stylist balance. Manual overrides are necessary because:
- Data import errors from affiliate networks
- Network API gaps where certain data is not automatically reported
- Reconciliation adjustments when actual payments do not match expected amounts
- Edge cases the automated rules cannot handle

Order Snapshots and Corrections

For Rakuten and Partnerize orders, TheFloorr keeps historical snapshots — records of what the order data looked like at each network report. These are used to track how orders change over time and to detect returns.

For CJ orders, adjustments come as separate "correction" records with negative values representing returns or chargebacks. These corrections are separate line items rather than changes to the original order data.

Notifications

When an order is received, the system creates notifications for the relevant shopper and/or stylist. These include the order amount, commission, network, and advertiser. Notifications can be delivered through email, push notification, SMS, or WhatsApp depending on user preferences.

Teams and Sales Targets

Stylists can be organised into teams. Each team member can have monthly and annual sales targets set in a specific currency. Team managers can view dashboards showing each member's performance against their targets.

Stylists on TheFloorr are called FRI (Fashion Retail Individual) or GPS (Guest Personal Shopper). FRI is the standard stylist role, and GPS refers to guest stylists who are invited for specific projects.

Stylists on TheFloorr are now called PSE (Personal Shopper Entrepreneur). The old names FRI (Fashion Retail Individual) and GPS (Guest Personal Shopper) are deprecated and no longer used. From the system's perspective, FRI, GPS, and PSE are the same thing.

Stylists can create Looks and Consultations (also called Styling Sessions) for their clients. A Look is a curated outfit combination, and a Consultation captures the client's needs including sizing, inspiration, and due dates. Stylists submit and publish Looks within Consultations.

The Looks and Consultations features are deprecated and no longer available in the system. PSE's direct communication with clients through the platform is also deprecated. Currently, PSEs are purchasing products themselves or sharing trackable links and moodboards publicly.
When a customer returns all items from an order, the order status is set to 'rejected' in the system. Rejected orders generate no commission for the stylist.

'Rejected' is an old deprecated name for 'returned' in TheFloorr's order system. Always use 'returned' instead of 'rejected' when describing orders where items were sent back. The internal status reflects this: if all items have been returned, the order is internally marked as returned.

questions.question: What is TheFloorr and how does it make money?
questions.expectedAnswer: TheFloorr is a luxury fashion personal styling platform that connects professional stylists with fashion-conscious clients. It does not sell products directly. Instead, it earns revenue through affiliate commissions when purchases are made through trackable links generated on the platform. Both TheFloorr and the stylist earn a share of the commission.

questions.question: What affiliate networks does TheFloorr use for its product catalogue?
questions.expectedAnswer: TheFloorr uses three affiliate networks: Rakuten, Commission Junction (CJ), and Partnerize. These networks provide product data feeds from hundreds of retailers, which are downloaded multiple times a day through an automated pipeline.

questions.question: What is a Moodboard on TheFloorr?
questions.expectedAnswer: A Moodboard is a curated product collection used for inspiration. Stylists create them to organise ideas for clients. Moodboards can be public or private, shared with specific users, and used as templates for teams. They contain products from the catalogue along with notes and inspiration.

questions.question: How does a stylist earn commission on TheFloorr? Walk me through the process.
questions.expectedAnswer: A stylist recommends products to clients using Moodboards or by sharing trackable links. When a client clicks a trackable link and purchases the product at the retailer's website, the affiliate network records the sale. Commission is generated and attributed to the stylist. The default split is 80% to the stylist and 20% to TheFloorr. Commission rates vary by retailer and can differ for full-price versus sale items.

questions.question: Why might a stylist's commission payment be delayed?
questions.expectedAnswer: Several reasons: (1) The affiliate network has not yet paid TheFloorr — the order's affiliate payment status is still pending. (2) The order is still pending approval at the network level. (3) The commission has not yet been approved for payment in the admin system. (4) The Revolut payment draft has not been created or sent yet.

questions.question: What happens when a customer returns one item from a multi-item order?
questions.expectedAnswer: Only the returned item's commission is affected. The order-level amounts are recalculated: the gross sale amount stays the same (representing the original order), but the net sale amount decreases by the returned item's value. The stylist still earns commission on the items that were kept. The order may show a 'mixed' status.

questions.question: What is attribution on TheFloorr and why does it matter?
questions.expectedAnswer: Attribution is the connection between a purchase and TheFloorr. When someone clicks a trackable link, TheFloorr records which product was clicked, who clicked it, and who created the link. If the product is bought, the affiliate network matches the sale back to that click, allowing TheFloorr to attribute the order to the right stylist for commission purposes. If an order cannot be matched to a specific stylist or click, the commission goes entirely to TheFloorr rather than being split with a stylist.

questions.question: How do order statuses work on TheFloorr? What are the different status dimensions?
questions.expectedAnswer: Every order has three separate, independent status tracks: (1) Affiliate Order Status — what the affiliate network reports (pending, new, approved, auto-approved, returned, declined, closed, locked, mixed). (2) Internal Order Status — TheFloorr's own assessment, calculated from affiliate status and item states. (3) Manual Status — an admin override that forces the internal status regardless of network data. These three dimensions move independently.

questions.question: A client buys a 500 pound dress from a retailer that pays 10% commission. The stylist's rate is 80%. If the client returns the dress after the affiliate network has already paid TheFloorr, what happens financially?
questions.expectedAnswer: Total commission was 50 pounds (10% of 500). Stylist's share was 40 pounds (80% of 50), TheFloorr's share was 10 pounds. After return: the affiliate network does not send money back — instead it deducts 50 pounds from the next month's payment to TheFloorr. If the stylist's 40 pounds has already been paid, the overpayment is tracked against the stylist's balance and deducted from future earnings.

questions.question: How does order handling differ between Rakuten and CJ networks on TheFloorr?
questions.expectedAnswer: Key differences: (1) Approval: Rakuten has no explicit approval step — an order is considered approved only when Rakuten actually pays the commission. CJ has a standard approval/rejection flow. (2) Returns tracking: For Rakuten, TheFloorr keeps historical snapshots of what the order looked like at each report. For CJ, returns come as separate 'correction' records with negative values, rather than changes to the original order. (3) Payment records: each network has its own payment record table since data formats differ.

questions.question: What is the difference between affiliate order status, internal order status, and manual status? Give examples of each.
questions.expectedAnswer: Affiliate Order Status reflects what the network reports: pending, new, approved, auto-approved, returned, declined, closed, locked, or mixed. Internal Order Status is TheFloorr's own calculation: if all items returned → internally returned; if network says closed/locked → internally approved; if still new/pending → stays pending. Manual Status is an admin override that forces internal status regardless of network data — used for data corrections, reconciliation, and edge cases the automated rules can't handle.

questions.question: What features and naming conventions on TheFloorr are deprecated and should no longer be used?
questions.expectedAnswer: Deprecated items: (1) FRI (Fashion Retail Individual) and GPS (Guest Personal Shopper) are deprecated names for stylists — now called PSE (Personal Shopper Entrepreneur). (2) Looks and Consultations (Styling Sessions) features are deprecated and no longer available. (3) PSE's direct communication with clients through the platform is deprecated. (4) The term 'rejected' for order status is deprecated — use 'returned' instead.

questions.question: Walk through all the stages a PSE payment goes through from order to bank account.
questions.expectedAnswer: The stages are: (1) Order is approved by the affiliate network. (2) The affiliate network pays TheFloorr the commission on its own schedule (typically monthly). (3) Commission is calculated for the stylist (default 80% of order commission). (4) An admin or the system approves the commission for payment, creating an 'approved payment' record. (5) Multiple approved payments are batched into a single Revolut payment draft. (6) The draft moves through states: approved, created in Revolut, sent, then completed or failed. (7) The stylist receives the money via bank transfer through Revolut. If VAT-registered in the UK, VAT is added on top.

questions.question: Why are manual adjustments necessary in TheFloorr's order system?
questions.expectedAnswer: Manual adjustments are necessary because: (1) Data import errors from affiliate networks. (2) Network API gaps where certain data is not automatically reported. (3) Reconciliation adjustments when actual payments don't match expected amounts. (4) Edge cases the automated rules cannot handle. Admins can manually override the internal status, payment statuses for all three streams, commission amounts, and stylist balance.

questions.question: How are products organized in TheFloorr's catalogue across regions and genders?
questions.expectedAnswer: Products are organised into sixteen search partitions based on gender (female, male) and region (UK, US, EU, Rest of World), with separate partitions for full-price and sale items. This means the same product may appear with different pricing depending on the customer's region. 2 genders x 4 regions x 2 price types = 16 partitions.

questions.question: How does the affiliate network handle commission refunds when a customer returns an item?
questions.expectedAnswer: Networks do not send money back to TheFloorr for returns. Instead, they deduct the returned commission from future payments. For example, if TheFloorr was owed 100 pounds in commission next month but a previous order had a 20 pound return after network payment, the network would pay 80 pounds. The refund status is marked as pending if return happened after the affiliate network already paid commission to TheFloorr.
